title: The Texas Screwworm Cases Are a Wake-Up Call for U.S. Biosecurity
gist: A RAND biosecurity researcher uses the June 2026 reemergence of New World screwworm in Texas to argue that U.S. crop agriculture, especially the Corn Belt, is dangerously underprotected against natural and engineered pathogens, and proposes leveraging existing CDC/USDA surveillance and rapid-response infrastructure as cheap insurance against tail-risk losses that would dwarf the prevention cost.

insights.claim: New World screwworm, a flesh-eating parasite eradicated from the U.S. ~60 years ago, was reconfirmed in livestock and pets on June 3, 2026, with 15 cases across Texas and southeastern New Mexico within three weeks.
insights.speaker: Tyler Hoard
insights.so_what: Live, escalating animal-health event in U.S. cattle country; watch beef supply chains, cattle futures, and USDA quarantine zones for disruption.
insights.quote: Within three weeks, 15 cases in livestock and pets have been confirmed across Texas and southeastern New Mexico.
insights.locator: The U.S. Department of Agriculture confirmed June 3

insights.claim: The screwworm response worked only because the USDA had a decades-old playbook of quarantine zones, trapping, sterile-fly releases, and pre-approved emergency drugs; the author argues no comparable playbook exists for crop pathogens.
insights.speaker: Tyler Hoard
insights.so_what: The system is only prepared for rehearsed threats; an off-script crop pathogen would find no equivalent containment apparatus.
insights.quote: And screwworm is the threat we rehearsed for. What happens when the next threat arrives, one that doesn't come with a playbook?
insights.locator: Still, we were unable to prevent

insights.claim: The U.S. Corn Belt produces more than one-third of all U.S. corn and 34 percent of the world's soybeans, with combined export value exceeding $34 billion.
insights.speaker: Tyler Hoard
insights.so_what: Quantifies the concentrated economic exposure a single crop-pathogen event could threaten; relevant to grain export, ag-input, and crop-insurance risk.
insights.quote: Their combined export value exceeds $34 billion.
insights.locator: The Corn Belt produces more than one-third

insights.claim: Tens of millions of acres are planted with a small number of high-yield varieties in rotation, making U.S. row-crop agriculture efficient but genetically fragile.
insights.speaker: Tyler Hoard
insights.so_what: Genetic uniformity is the transmission mechanism that turns a localized outbreak into a systemic crop failure.
insights.quote: This kind of farming is efficient but also fragile.

insights.claim: In the 1970s, with a single genetic strain dominating commercial corn, Southern corn leaf blight destroyed 15 percent of the North American crop, and crop-strain concentration is by some measures more pronounced today.
insights.speaker: Tyler Hoard
insights.so_what: Historical precedent showing a double-digit percentage crop loss is achievable; current concentration implies the tail risk has grown, not shrunk.
insights.quote: an outbreak of Southern corn leaf blight destroyed 15 percent (PDF) of the North American crop
insights.locator: In the 1970s, when a single genetic strain

insights.claim: Agricultural sabotage historically requires little sophistication: in 1989 cyanide-laced Chilean grapes triggered a U.S. import suspension that cost Chile's ag sector $330 million.
insights.speaker: Tyler Hoard
insights.so_what: A handful of tampered units can crater an entire export channel via import bans, not just direct contamination losses.
insights.quote: In 1989, terrorists laced a few grapes exported from Chile to the United States with cyanide. The import suspension that followed cost Chile's agriculture sector $330 million.
insights.locator: Further, history shows that agricultural sabotage

insights.claim: In 1978 a Palestinian group claimed responsibility for injecting mercury into Israeli oranges exported to Europe, after which European orange imports from Israel fell by 40 percent.
insights.speaker: Tyler Hoard
insights.so_what: Demonstrates the durable demand and trade-flow damage from a single deliberate contamination claim.
insights.quote: European orange imports from Israel fell by 40 percent (PDF).

insights.claim: Advances in synthetic biology mean novel pathogens could be engineered to kill crops, and if released covertly during a growing season could go undetected for weeks, long enough to spread beyond any containable perimeter.
insights.speaker: Tyler Hoard
insights.so_what: Deliberate, engineered crop bioterror is now technically plausible and may exceed natural-disease losses; an emerging national-security risk category.
insights.quote: Released covertly during a growing season, it could go undetected for weeks—long enough to spread beyond any containable perimeter.

insights.claim: RAND research published spring 2026 found the U.S. biosecurity system is poorly prepared for crop diseases not yet present domestically, which could cause billions in losses, with engineered deliberate pathogens potentially far worse.
insights.speaker: Tyler Hoard
insights.so_what: Frames an under-covered, large-magnitude national risk; potential driver of future USDA/DHS biosecurity spending.
insights.quote: Naturally occurring crop diseases not yet present in the United States could cause billions of dollars in losses if introduced here.
insights.locator: In research published this spring by RAND

insights.claim: The U.S. monitors human pathogens (e.g., CDC wastewater surveillance for COVID-19) but crop-pathogen screening such as the National Plant Diagnostic Network is minimal, and there is no crop-equivalent of the screwworm playbook.
insights.speaker: Tyler Hoard
insights.so_what: Identifies a concrete capability gap; companies in biosurveillance, rapid diagnostics, and ag-biotech could benefit if policy closes it.
insights.quote: But screening for new crop pathogens, such as the National Plant Diagnostic Network, is minimal.
insights.locator: The United States performs disease surveillance

insights.claim: The author proposes four low-cost fixes leveraging existing systems: extend CDC-style wastewater surveillance to ag pathogens, fund rapid field crop diagnostics for extension agents, create a USDA RAPID-style rapid-response research mechanism, and build forensic attribution capability across USDA, CDC, State, Justice and possibly CIA.
insights.speaker: Tyler Hoard
insights.so_what: A concrete policy roadmap signaling where future federal ag-biosecurity dollars may flow (diagnostics, surveillance, forensics).
insights.quote: Closing the gap doesn't require building from scratch. It just requires leveraging systems that already exist.
insights.locator: Closing the gap doesn't require building

insights.claim: Rapid forensic attribution matters because determining whether an outbreak is natural or deliberate dictates a fundamentally different response, requiring interagency collaboration up to the CIA.
insights.speaker: Tyler Hoard
insights.so_what: Attribution capability is a missing link; its absence could delay or misdirect response to a deliberate agro-attack.
insights.quote: When the next outbreak occurs, we will need to determine quickly whether it was natural or deliberate. The response to each is fundamentally different.
insights.locator: And finally, we must invest in forensic

insights.claim: The author argues prevention is asymmetric insurance: a single novel-pathogen introduction reducing yields by even a fraction of the worst-case would dwarf the cost of the surveillance and research infrastructure needed to prevent it.
insights.speaker: Tyler Hoard
insights.so_what: Classic low-probability/high-consequence insurance logic; supports the case for proactive ag-biosecurity budget even under thin producer margins.
insights.quote: We insure against low-probability, high-consequence events in every other domain of national security
